prompt_text: Explain the concept of compound interest to Darnell. Help Darnell understand how investments grow over time.
response_text:
Hey Darnell, let's talk about compound interest. Imagine you have a savings account where you deposit $1,000. At the end of the first year, you earn a 5% interest rate, which means you'll have $1,050 in your account. That's a $50 gain.

Now, here's the key part: the next year, you don't just earn 5% interest on the original $1,000. You earn 5% interest on the new balance of $1,050. So, in the second year, you'll earn 5% of $1,050, which is $52.50. Now your account balance is $1,102.50.

As you can see, the interest earned in the second year is more than the first year, even though the interest rate remains the same. This is because the interest is being applied to a larger balance, which is the result of the previous year's interest.

This process continues year after year, with the interest being applied to the new balance each time. This is called compound interest, and it's a powerful way to grow your investments over time.

To illustrate this further, let's look at an example:

| Year | Balance | Interest Earned | New Balance |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | $1,000 | $50 | $1,050 |
| 2 | $1,050 | $52.50 | $1,102.50 |
| 3 | $1,102.50 | $55.13 | $1,157.63 |
| 4 | $1,157.63 | $57.88 | $1,215.51 |
| 5 | $1,215.51 | $60.78 | $1,276.29 |

As you can see, the balance grows rapidly over time, even with a relatively low interest rate. This is the power of compound interest, and it's a key concept to understand when it comes to investing.

So, Darnell, I hope this helps you understand how investments can grow over time with compound interest. It's a powerful tool that can help you achieve your financial goals, but it's essential to remember that it takes time and patience to see the results.
